video_title: Most Investors Fear Uncertainty, But Systematic Traders Don’t | Systematic Investor | Ep. 406
gist: Mark Rzepczynski and Niels Kaastrup-Larsen review H1 2026 trend-following performance and the macro/structural regime changes ahead — chiefly new Fed chair Kevin Warsh's five task forces (forward guidance, balance sheet, data quality, inflation, AI productivity) — then present Mark's hedge-fund research showing managed futures is uniquely insensitive to high-VIX risk regimes and risk shocks while most other strategies' returns, alpha and beta deteriorate.

insights.claim: Individual Fed chairs exert outsized, decisive influence on the US and global economy, so the 'key man' matters far more than depersonalized institutional narratives suggest.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Treat a Fed chair transition as a potential structural regime change worth repositioning around, not a continuity event.
insights.quote: people at the Fed do matter

insights.claim: Greenspan's failure to recognize or accept an asset bubble after cutting rates near 1% laid the foundation for the housing boom and subsequent Great Financial Crisis.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: A reminder that prolonged ultra-low rates can seed the next crisis; watch for similar complacency under new leadership.
insights.quote: he was not able to identify or accept that there was a bubble after he lowered interest rates, you know, to close to 1% which really sort of exploded the housing market

insights.claim: Historically, the S&P 500 has lost ground in the first three months of every new Fed chair's tenure across roughly 10-12 chairs, with Greenspan the worst at -33% and Bernanke the best at only -2%.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Implies near-term equity downside risk as Warsh takes over; no chair transition in the sample saw equities rise in the first 3 months.
insights.quote: Alan Greenspan I had said he actually had the worst performance. The S&P 500 lost 33% in the first 3 months of his tenure

insights.claim: The 1994 surprise rate hike — the first in roughly five years — inflicted severe pain on fixed-income and trend-following managers, illustrating how an abrupt policy shift can blindside positioning.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Abrupt, unsignaled policy turns are a recurring source of drawdown risk; current low forward guidance raises that tail risk again.
insights.quote: I had a lot of pain right after that announcement. So that that was one of the most painful periods of my fixed income career

insights.claim: In any given year most trend-following profit comes from only a handful of markets, so the edge lies in capturing the few outsized moves rather than broad diversification per se.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Investors should judge CTAs on their ability to size into and risk-manage the few aberrant moves, not on breadth of markets alone.
insights.quote: if you look back over a given year, most of the money will be only made in a few markets

insights.claim: The right number of traded markets is a middle ground: enough to ensure exposure when an unpredictable market makes a large move, but not so many that position sizing dilutes exposure to the markets that actually move.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Frames the replicator (10-12 markets) vs large-manager (50-300 markets) debate as a sizing/liquidity trade-off, not a binary.
insights.quote: capturing and um trading enough markets so you get the exposure when you have these large aberrant moves. At the same time, not trade too much too many markets

insights.claim: Trading only crude oil for energy exposure leaves money on the table: over the trailing 12 months crude rose 37% while kerosene/heating oil rose 130%.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Concrete case for trading the full energy complex rather than a single liquid proxy when sub-sector moves diverge sharply.
insights.quote: crude oil, yes, it's up. It's up 37%. That's great. But, it's kerosene heating oil is up 130%. So, you left something on the table by only trading crude oil

insights.claim: Trend followers should covet rising cross-market correlation during shocks because synchronized moves create a multiplier effect across positions; only the absolute correlation matters, not its sign, since one can be short one market and long another.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Reframes 'diversification' for CTAs: shock-driven correlation is a profit engine, so portfolio construction should target convexity to correlated shocks.
insights.quote: you actually like when markets start to get correlated because then it increases uh the the shock effect from a given shock

insights.claim: Replicators can mimic trend-following returns via regression to infer correlated exposures with few markets, but a genuine trend follower running its own system needs a much broader market set to manage risk and find opportunities.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Distinguishes cheap replication products from true CTAs; the gap shows up in tail capture and risk management, not headline correlation.

insights.claim: Brent crude is trading at four-month lows as the Strait of Hormuz normalizes and more tankers pass through, marking an energy regime change that has pressured trend-following performance.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: The geopolitical-premium reversal in energy is a fresh trend that hurt CTAs riding the earlier run-up; watch for the new short-side trend.
insights.quote: Brent now trades at 4-month lows. And anticipation of more flows

insights.claim: Gold has fallen from roughly 5,200 to near 4,000 — a large move down despite ongoing world conflict — defying the typical safe-haven expectation.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Gold's counterintuitive sell-off amid conflict is a tradable trend reversal and a caution against assuming geopolitical risk lifts gold.
insights.quote: you look at we've gone from like 5,200 down to, you know, now we're close to 4,000. Huge move

insights.claim: The US dollar, recently 'getting bashed,' has flipped into a strong upward trend, surprising commentators given the macro backdrop.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: A nascent dollar uptrend is a new trend-following opportunity and a headwind for non-USD assets if it persists.
insights.quote: The dollar was it was getting bashed and now all of a sudden we're starting to have a strong upward trend

insights.claim: The US 10-year yield has been range-bound near 4–4.5% for almost a year, bouncing higher below 4% and fading back above 4.5%, making bonds the exception to clean trends.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: A choppy, mean-reverting rates market is poor terrain for trend models in fixed income; the profits come from commodities and FX instead.
insights.quote: the 10-year has been trading between 4 4 and 1/2 4 middle almost a year

insights.claim: CTA indices are up roughly double digits year-to-date (e.g. short-term trend index +10.88%, trend index +10.6%, beta-50 +9.67%) while the MSCI World is +8.26% and S&P 500 total return +8.22%.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Trend following is keeping pace with or beating equities YTD with uncorrelated return drivers — a live diversification case.
insights.quote: Up 10.88 so far this year

insights.claim: June was a corrective month for trend followers, with silver down 19% month-to-date and meaningful corrections in equities, metals and energy pushing CTAs slightly negative on the month.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Recent CTA softness is a cross-asset corrective month, not a strategy breakdown — context for assessing drawdowns.
insights.quote: silver months to date down 19% quite a lot

insights.claim: For trend followers, the only macro question that matters is whether regime changes or dislocations will create longer-term trends — they follow prices but watch for the conditions that generate them.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Allocators should raise managed-futures weight specifically when they anticipate regime changes likely to produce sustained trends.
insights.quote: are there going to be regime changes or dislocations in the market that could lead to longer-term trends?

insights.claim: Regime changes split into structural (e.g. a new Fed chair imprinting policy) and behavioral (the market's response to a shock), both defined as economic adjustments that produce longer-term trends.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: A usable taxonomy for diagnosing whether a current event is likely to spawn a durable trend worth allocating to.

insights.claim: Higher uncertainty causes market participants to dollar-cost-average — bleeding capital in or out rather than jumping — which slows price adjustment to new equilibria and creates the time dimension trend followers exploit.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Explains why elevated uncertainty structurally favors trend following: slower repricing extends the duration of trends.
insights.quote: if you have a regime where there's high uncertainty, then there's going to be more caution in behavior. If there's more cautious in behavior, then the price adjustment to a new equilibrium will take time

insights.claim: The spread of NLP and large language models is itself a potential regime change, converting soft/qualitative information from earnings calls and Fed statements into hard numerical signals and altering how markets respond to announcements.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: LLM-driven sentiment extraction may compress reaction times and change market microstructure — a structural shift for systematic modeling.
insights.quote: they're trying to now convert soft information into numerical information or hard information. And that extraction of doing that is changing the way markets respond

themes.theme: Warsh Fed & Monetary Policy Regime Shift

insights.claim: New Fed chair Kevin Warsh is proving less dovish than the market anticipated when President Trump nominated him expecting reduced independence and lower rates.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Positioning for an easy-money Warsh regime may be wrong-footed; rate cuts could be slower than priced.
insights.quote: it doesn't seem that he's as dovish a Fed chairman as what was been perceived

insights.claim: The Fed is effectively operating to a 3–4% inflation range — raising if inflation hits 4%, facing pressure to cut below 3% — despite the official 2% target.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: If true, the reaction function sits well above 2%, implying a higher structural rate floor than consensus assumes.
insights.quote: surprisingly with all the talk about a 2% inflation target, we're actually running, you know, policy based on a range between 3 and 4%

insights.claim: Warsh has set up five Fed task forces — forward guidance, balance sheet, Fed data quality, inflation stability, and AI productivity — signaling the agenda of his structural regime change.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: These five areas map the likely policy shifts; each carries distinct implications for rates, bonds and systematic strategies.
insights.quote: Chairman Warsh talked about five task force that he's now going to in has set up within the Fed

insights.claim: Warsh's dislike of forward guidance — refusing to submit a dot for the dot plot and calling it 'in pencil, not pen' — signals the dot plot is on its way out.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Less forward guidance means prices driven by fundamentals and actions, reducing Fed-speech noise — a tailwind for trend following.
insights.quote: if the chairman doesn't give you a dot for the dot plots, how much longer is the dot plots going to be around

insights.claim: Reduced forward guidance is good for trend followers because prices will be driven by fundamentals and actions rather than speeches, making price signals less noisy.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: A cleaner price signal regime improves trend-model efficacy; forward guidance only really helped at the zero bound.
insights.quote: less forward guidance may be good for trend followers. It's going to mean that prices aren't going to be as noisy going forward

insights.claim: Shrinking the Fed's '800-lb gorilla' balance sheet would force federal debt to be financed by private or official-reserve buyers, likely clearing at higher yields than if the Fed were buying.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: A smaller Fed footprint implies structurally higher bond yields and a supply/demand-driven bond market — bearish duration.
insights.quote: if you don't have an accommodative Fed with a large balance sheet, then that means that the federal debt in the US is going to have to be financed from other sources

insights.claim: Even if the Fed stops buying bonds, someone must absorb the heavy supply — likely banks pushed via rule changes — so the question is the clearing yield, not whether bonds get bought.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen
insights.so_what: Watch for regulatory tweaks nudging banks to hold more Treasuries as a backdoor financing mechanism for the deficit.
insights.quote: someone is going to be asked to buy whatever the Fed is not going to buy

insights.claim: Deteriorating US economic data quality — driven by falling survey response rates and large BLS revisions — raises the relative value of pure price-based systems over fundamental ones in the short run.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: In a poor-data environment, price-only trend models outperform quasi-fundamental approaches; allocate accordingly.
insights.quote: the value of the person who just focuses on price is probably is going to be more valuable in this in a poor data environment

insights.claim: Fed policy has run inflation above its 2% target for five-plus years, a striking reversal from Bernanke and Yellen who argued deflation, not inflation, was the harder-to-solve threat.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Persistent above-target inflation undercuts the deflation-fear policy framework and supports a higher-for-longer rate stance.
insights.quote: we're 5 years out and we're still you know, above target inflation

insights.claim: An AI-driven productivity boom — echoing the 1990s Greenspan productivity surge — could let the Fed keep policy looser than otherwise and is important because global growth is currently relatively poor.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: AI productivity gains are a swing factor for both growth and the rate path; effect on trend following judged neutral for now.
insights.quote: if we do have an AI productivity boom, then what happens is is that monetary policy could be held a little bit looser

insights.claim: Macro trends drive price trends, so a 'trend follower plus fundamentalist' uses fundamentals as a supporting indicator of price direction, while a pure trend follower relies on prices alone because fundamental data is more backward-looking.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Defines the design choice between pure-price and hybrid systems; hybrids add confirmation but lag on fresh information.
insights.quote: macro trends will drive price trends. Hence, you want to look at fundamentals to be able to give you a support or another indication on what will be the direction of prices

insights.claim: Combining a price-based system with a separate fundamental system and systematically weighting the two lowers volatility and smooths returns, with weights automatically reduced when one system's quality declines.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete answer to handling novel economic data regimes: adaptive weighting rather than switching a model off.
insights.quote: if you systematically weight the two of them, then the combination will be better. You'll have you'll lower volatility and have a smoother, you know, return pattern

insights.claim: The current optimal weighting on fundamental systems is lower than historically observed, shifting allocation toward price-based systems.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Signals that in today's poor-data environment, models are mechanically de-emphasizing fundamentals in favor of price.
insights.quote: the weighting on the fundamental system has been lower than what I've seen in the past when I look at at models

insights.claim: Survey and confidence data show a weak economy — elevated inflation expectations, low business optimism, and Conference Board consumer confidence near 2020 lows — casting doubt on a second-half equity rally.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Soft data argues against continued equity gains into H2, a caution for risk-on positioning.
insights.quote: consumer confidence if you look at the conference board uh numbers are closer to 2020 as opposed to a high period

themes.theme: Hedge Fund Behavior Across VIX Risk Regimes

insights.claim: Competition for hedge-fund allocations should be framed broadly across all 10-plus strategy types, not narrowly CTA-vs-CTA — analogous to entertainment competing across sports and movies, not just football team vs football team.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Allocators should compare managed futures against the full alternatives menu on risk behavior, not just peer CTAs.

insights.claim: Mark's research decomposes hedge-fund sensitivity to two VIX dimensions — the risk regime (VIX level: low/medium/high) and risk shocks (the change in VIX) — testing whether managers truly handle uncertainty skillfully.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Provides a two-axis lens (level vs change in volatility) for evaluating any strategy's true uncertainty resilience.

insights.claim: In high-risk (high-VIX) regimes, most hedge fund strategies' excess returns diminish significantly with a strong negative impact even after accounting for beta.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Most alternatives fail exactly when investors need protection most; their diversification evaporates in stress.
insights.quote: if we look at a high risk regime, uh you know, excess returns diminish significantly. There's a strong negative impact

insights.claim: Managed futures' excess returns are insensitive to high-risk regimes and indifferent to high risk shocks — losing nothing where other strategies lose money — making it the standout uncertainty diversifier.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Core actionable conclusion: increase managed-futures allocation if you expect a high-uncertainty, high-VIX environment.
insights.quote: Managed futures is insensitive to high risk shocks. So it doesn't mean that it is it's making money from it, but where others are losing money, the managed futures actually is indifferent to that

insights.claim: The positive alpha many hedge fund strategies show in low-to-medium risk turns negative in high-risk regimes and against high risk shocks; managed futures shows no alpha component in either case.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Alpha investors rely on disappears precisely in crises; don't count on other strategies' skill premium when the VIX spikes.
insights.quote: if you're thinking that you're going to get alpha from other hedge fund strategies in high risk regimes, that's not going to be the the case

insights.claim: In high-risk regimes and high risk shocks, managed futures' beta turns more negative and significant, while many other strategies' beta rises — increasing their sensitivity to systematic risk exactly when stress hits.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Managed futures gets more defensive in stress while peers get more correlated to equities — the crisis-alpha case for CTAs.
insights.quote: for high risk regimes, the the beta actually gets uh gets to be negative and significant

insights.claim: Simply bundling many alternative strategies together can work in benign conditions but is not the best way to protect against high-risk regimes and risk shocks.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Diversification-by-bundling fails as crisis protection; deliberate allocation to convex strategies like managed futures is required.
insights.quote: if you're trying to sort of protect yourself against you know, risk high risk regimes and risk shocks, that's may not be the best strategy

insights.claim: Equity market neutral is a surprising exception that may perform somewhat better in certain shock environments compared with most other strategies.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: A potential second uncertainty diversifier beyond managed futures, worth further scrutiny when building stress-resilient portfolios.
insights.quote: I found equity market neutral is in terms of the structure of when we have shocks, this is that they they might do a little bit better in some in some environments

insights.claim: The past 12 months of elevated uncertainty validate the thesis: VIX shocks correlated with improved managed-futures performance while other hedge fund strategies struggled in the high-volatility environment.
insights.speaker: Mark Rzepczynski
insights.so_what: Recent live evidence supports raising CTA weight as an uncertainty hedge, though one episode shouldn't be over-extrapolated.
insights.quote: we had a big shock to the VIX index, you know, and that was sort of correlated when the performance for managed futures actually improved
